exercises.title: Add toString to a class
exercises.instructions: Fill in the blanks to override toString.
hints: @Override annotation tells compiler 'this overrides an inherited method'
toString returns String
println(c) calls c.toString() implicitly

exercises.title: Default vs overridden toString
hints: println calls toString implicitly
B overrides toString, returns custom message
Output: 'Hello from B'

exercises.title: Person with toString
exercises.scenario: Override toString in a multi-field class.
exercises.instructions:
Create a `Person` class with private fields name (String) and age (int). Constructor takes both. Override toString to return 'Person[name=<name>, age=<age>]'.

In main, print 2 persons.

Expected output:
Person[name=Aisha, age=28]
Person[name=Raj, age=35]
exercises.starter_code: // Build Person class with toString override
hints: toString returns String with field values
Use string concatenation: 'Person[name=' + name + ', age=' + age + ']'
@Override annotation on the method

exercises.title: Override equals (single field)
exercises.scenario: Implement equals using the standard pattern.
exercises.instructions:
Create a `Product` class with private final int id and private String name. Override equals to compare by id only. Don't worry about hashCode yet — we'll cover the contract next.

In main, create two products with same id (different names), verify equals returns true.

Expected output:
Same id: true
Different id: false
exercises.starter_code:
// Build Product class with equals override
// Compare by id only

test_cases.expected_stdout:
Same id: true
Different id: false

hints: Follow the template: == check, instanceof check, cast, compare
Parameter type is Object (not Product)
Use instanceof to check type AND handle null
Cast: Product p = (Product) other
Compare: this.id == p.id

exercises.title: Fix the wrong equals signature
exercises.scenario: equals must take Object, not specific type.
exercises.instructions:
This code COMPILES but equals doesn't work. Fix the signature.

Expected output:
Equals: true

bugs.issue: equals(Box other) doesn't override Object.equals(Object). When called with Object reference, the inherited method is used (reference equality).
bugs.fix: Change parameter to Object, add instanceof check, cast inside

hints: Original equals takes Box — this is a NEW method, not an override
Without @Override annotation, the bug isn't caught at compile time
When called via Object reference, default equals is used (returns false)
Fix: parameter must be Object, add @Override, instanceof check, cast

exercises.title: Override hashCode with Objects.hash
exercises.instructions:
Take the Product class from earlier exercises. Override hashCode using Objects.hash(id). Test that two equal Products have equal hashCodes.

Expected output:
Equals: true
Same hashCode: true
hints: Import java.util.Objects at top
hashCode returns int, no parameters
Use @Override annotation
Return Objects.hash(id) — same field as equals uses
Two products with same id → equal AND same hashCode

exercises.title: Fix the missing hashCode
exercises.scenario: Equals overridden without hashCode — contract violated.
exercises.instructions:
This class overrides equals but NOT hashCode. Fix it.

Expected output:
Equal: true
Same hashCode: true

bugs.issue: equals is overridden but hashCode is not. The contract is violated — equal objects have different hashCodes (from default Object.hashCode based on memory address).
bugs.fix: Add hashCode() using the same fields as equals: Objects.hash(x, y)

hints: Equals returns true for equal objects
But hashCode returns different values (default implementation uses memory address)
This violates the contract
Add @Override public int hashCode() { return Objects.hash(x, y); }
Same fields as equals: x and y

exercises.title: Multi-field equals with Objects.equals
exercises.instructions:
Create a `Book` class with private fields title (String) and author (String). Override equals to compare BOTH fields. Use Objects.equals for null-safe String comparison. Also override hashCode.

In main:
- 2 books with same title/author → equals true, same hashCode
- 2 books with different titles → equals false

Expected output:
Same data: true / same hash: true
Different data: false
exercises.starter_code:
import java.util.Objects;

// Build Book class with equals/hashCode using BOTH title and author

test_cases.expected_stdout:
Same data: true / same hash: true
Different data: false

hints: Compare both title AND author using Objects.equals (null-safe)
Use && to combine the two checks
Why Objects.equals not .equals()? Because if title is null, this.title.equals(...) crashes. Objects.equals handles null.
hashCode uses BOTH fields: Objects.hash(title, author)

exercises.title: Complete value class with all three methods
exercises.scenario: Put it all together: toString, equals, hashCode.
exercises.instructions:
Create a `Money` class with private final double amount and private final String currency. Add a constructor, then override all THREE methods:
- toString: returns 'amount currency' like '100.0 USD'
- equals: same amount AND same currency
- hashCode: based on both

In main, create two equal Money objects and one different. Print all three results.

Expected output:
100.0 USD
m1.equals(m2): true
m1.hashCode == m2.hashCode: true
m1.equals(m3): false
exercises.starter_code:
import java.util.Objects;

// Build complete Money class with toString, equals, hashCode

test_cases.expected_stdout:
100.0 USD
m1.equals(m2): true
m1.hashCode == m2.hashCode: true
m1.equals(m3): false

hints: toString: amount + ' ' + currency
equals: check amount (use Double.compare for safety) AND currency (Objects.equals)
hashCode: Objects.hash(amount, currency)
Three @Override annotations
Same currency string ↔ same comparison

exercises.title: Trace the equals template
exercises.scenario: Predict the result of various equals comparisons.
hints: a.equals(b): different objects, same id → true (id comparison)
a.equals(c): same reference → true (early return)
a.equals(null): instanceof null is always false → false
a.equals("hello"): "hello" is not an Item → false

exercises.title: Find duplicates in object array
exercises.scenario: Use equals to find duplicate objects.
exercises.instructions:
Create a `User` class with private final int id and private String name. Override equals (compare by id only) and hashCode.

Then write a method `countUnique(User[] users)` that returns the count of unique users (by id).

In main, create an array with some duplicates and print count.

Users: ids 1, 2, 1, 3, 2, 4 → unique count = 4 (ids 1, 2, 3, 4)

Expected output:
Unique count: 4
hints: User equals compares by id only
For each user, check if any EARLIER user equals it
If no earlier match, it's unique → count++
Use .equals() for User comparison
ids: 1, 2, 1(dup), 3, 2(dup), 4 → 4 unique
Later in Collections module, use HashSet — much easier

exercises.title: toString with all fields and computed value
exercises.instructions:
Create a `Rectangle` class with private final double width and height. Override toString to show width, height, AND the area (width × height).

Format: 'Rectangle[5.0 x 3.0, area=15.0]'

In main, create and print two rectangles.

Expected output:
Rectangle[5.0 x 3.0, area=15.0]
Rectangle[2.0 x 4.0, area=8.0]
exercises.starter_code: // Build Rectangle with toString that includes computed area

test_cases.expected_stdout:
Rectangle[5.0 x 3.0, area=15.0]
Rectangle[2.0 x 4.0, area=8.0]

hints: Method area() computes width * height
toString calls area() to include in output
Format: 'Rectangle[' + width + ' x ' + height + ', area=' + area() + ']'
Methods can call other methods of the same object freely

exercises.title: Coordinate class — full implementation
exercises.scenario: Real value class with all three methods + extras.
exercises.instructions:
Create a `Coordinate` class for 2D points:
- Private final fields: x (double), y (double)
- Constructor takes both
- Getters
- toString: '(x, y)' format
- equals: same x AND same y (use Double.compare)
- hashCode: Objects.hash(x, y)
- Method `distanceTo(Coordinate other)`: Euclidean distance using Math.sqrt and Math.pow

In main:
- Create 3 coordinates
- Print each
- Show equals comparison
- Print distance from (0,0) to (3,4) — should be 5.0

Expected output:
(0.0, 0.0)
(3.0, 4.0)
(0.0, 0.0)
c1.equals(c2): false
c1.equals(c3): true
Distance c1 to c2: 5.0
exercises.starter_code:
import java.util.Objects;

// Full Coordinate class with toString, equals, hashCode, distanceTo

test_cases.expected_stdout:
(0.0, 0.0)
(3.0, 4.0)
(0.0, 0.0)
c1.equals(c2): false
c1.equals(c3): true
Distance c1 to c2: 5.0

hints: All three Object methods overridden
equals uses Double.compare for floating-point safety
Distance: √(dx² + dy²)
3-4-5 right triangle: distance is 5
This is a complete production-quality value class

exercises.title: Fix the equals/hashCode mismatch
exercises.scenario: equals and hashCode use different fields — contract violation.
exercises.instructions:
This class has a subtle bug — equals checks two fields, but hashCode uses one. Fix it so both use the same fields.

Expected output:
Equal: true
Same hashCode: true

bugs.issue: equals uses BOTH id and department, but hashCode uses ONLY id. While THIS test case happens to work (same id), the bug strikes when two employees have same id but different departments — equal by mistake but with same hashCode.
bugs.fix: hashCode must use the SAME fields as equals: Objects.hash(id, department)

hints: equals checks id AND department
hashCode uses only id — this is the violation
Rule: same fields in equals and hashCode
Change hashCode to: Objects.hash(id, department)
Both methods now consistent

exercises.title: Address class with composition
exercises.scenario: Multi-field equals where some fields are objects.
exercises.instructions:
Create an `Address` class:
- Private fields: street (String), city (String), zip (String)
- Constructor + getters
- Full toString, equals, hashCode using ALL three fields

Then create a `Person` class:
- Private fields: name (String), address (Address)
- Constructor + getters
- toString, equals, hashCode using BOTH fields (note: address is an Object — Objects.equals handles it)

In main:
- Create 2 persons with identical name and identical addresses
- Verify they're equal AND have same hashCode
- Create a 3rd person with same name but different address — should NOT be equal

Expected output:
Person[name=Aisha, address=Address[123 MG Road, Hyderabad, 500001]]
p1.equals(p2): true
Same hashCode: true
p1.equals(p3): false

test_cases.expected_stdout:
Person[name=Aisha, address=Address[123 MG Road, Hyderabad, 500001]]
p1.equals(p2): true
Same hashCode: true
p1.equals(p3): false

hints: Both classes need full Object methods
Address.equals uses all 3 fields
Person.equals uses name and address (Object — use Objects.equals)
p1.equals(p2) works because Address.equals is properly overridden
Without Address.equals, Person.equals would compare by reference and fail
Composition: Person CONTAINS an Address — common pattern
